content:
# Acts Chapter 26 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 257-258 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_026_page_001.jpg, ACT_026_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And Agrippa said to Paul, "You are permitted to speak on your own behalf." Then Paul, having stretched out his hand, began to make his defense:

**2** "Concerning all the things of which I am accused by the Jews, King Agrippa, I consider myself blessed that I am about to make my defense before you today, **3** especially because you are well acquainted with all the customs and controversies among the Jews. Therefore I beg you to hear me patiently.

**4** "My manner of life from my youth, which from the beginning was spent among my own nation and in Jerusalem, all the Jews know, **5** having known me from the first -- if they are willing to testify -- that according to the strictest sect of our religion I lived as a Pharisee. **6** And now I stand being judged for the hope of the promise made by God to our fathers, **7** to which our twelve tribes, earnestly serving God night and day, hope to attain. Concerning this hope I am accused by the Jews, O king! **8** Why is it judged incredible among you if God raises the dead?

**9** "I myself indeed thought it necessary to do many things in opposition to the name of Jesus the Nazorean, **10** which I also did in Jerusalem. And many of the saints I shut up in prisons, having received authority from the chief priests; and when they were being put to death, I cast my vote against them. **11** And throughout all the synagogues, punishing them often, I was compelling them to blaspheme; and being exceedingly enraged against them, I was persecuting them even to foreign cities.

**12** "While so engaged, as I was journeying to Damascus with the authority and commission of the chief priests, **13** at midday along the road, O king, I saw a light from heaven, surpassing the brightness of the sun, shining around me and those journeying with me. **14** And when we had all fallen to the ground, I heard a voice saying to me in the Hebrew language, 'Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It is hard for you to kick against the goads.' **15** And I said, 'Who are you, Lord?' And the Lord said, 'I am Jesus whom you are persecuting. **16** But rise and stand upon your feet; for I have appeared to you for this purpose, to appoint you as a servant and a witness both of the things in which you have seen me and of the things in which I will appear to you, **17** delivering you from the people and from the Gentiles, to whom I am sending you, **18** to open their eyes, that they may turn from darkness to light and from the authority of Satan to God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins and an inheritance among those who have been sanctified by faith in me.'

**19** "Therefore, King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision, **20** but declared first to those in Damascus and then in Jerusalem, and throughout all the region of Judea, and to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God, doing works worthy of repentance. **21** On account of these things the Jews, having seized me in the temple, were attempting to kill me. **22** Having therefore obtained help from God, I stand to this day, testifying both to small and great, saying nothing beyond what both the prophets and Moses said would come to pass -- **23** that the Christ must suffer, and that being the first to rise from the dead, he would proclaim light both to the people and to the Gentiles."

**24** And as he was saying these things in his defense, Festus said with a loud voice, "You are mad, Paul! Your great learning is driving you to madness!" **25** But Paul said, "I am not mad, most excellent Festus, but I am speaking words of truth and soundness of mind. **26** For the king knows about these things, to whom also I speak with boldness. For I am persuaded that none of these things escapes his notice, for this has not been done in a corner. **27** King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know that you believe." **28** And Agrippa said to Paul, "In a short time you are persuading me to become a Christian!" **29** And Paul said, "I would pray to God, whether in a short time or a long time, that not only you but also all who hear me today might become such as I am -- except for these chains."

**30** And the king rose up, and the governor, and Bernice, and those sitting with them. **31** And having withdrawn, they were speaking to one another, saying, "This man is doing nothing worthy of death or of chains." **32** And Agrippa said to Festus, "This man could have been released if he had not appealed to Caesar."

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

All three translators agreed on the following procedural points, requiring no adjudication:

- **Verse 14:** All three preserve the Semitic name form "Saul, Saul" (EMET's "Saoul" being a stricter transliteration but phonetically equivalent) and the detail that the voice spoke in the Hebrew language. All include the goads proverb unique to the Acts 26 account.
- **Verse 15:** All three render the exchange identically in substance: "Who are you, Lord?" / "I am Jesus whom you are persecuting."
- **Verse 27:** All three render the rhetorical question and Paul's confident assertion identically in sense: "Do you believe the prophets? I know that you believe."
- **Verse 30:** All three agree on the sequence: the king, the governor, Bernice, and those sitting with them rose.
- **Manuscript verification:** Both folio images (pages 257-258) were examined. The text of Acts 26 is well-preserved in Sinaiticus with no significant lacunae. The readings confirmed include ΝΑΖΩΡΑΙΟΥ (v.9), ΔΩΔΕΚΑΦΥΛΟΝ (v.7), ΕΝ ΟΛΙΓΩ (v.28), and ΚΑΤΗΝΕΓΚΑ ΨΗΦΟΝ (v.10).

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | Opening verb and phrasing | "It-is-permitted for-you to-speak concerning yourself" | "You are permitted to speak on your own behalf" | "You have permission to speak for yourself" | Adopted LOGOS: "You are permitted to speak on your own behalf" -- closest to the Greek passive construction while remaining natural. |
| 1 | Paul's gesture | "having-stretched-out the hand" | "having stretched out his hand" | "raised his hand" | Adopted LOGOS: "having stretched out his hand." PNEUMA's "raised" loses the specific verb (ekteinas). EMET's "the hand" omits the possessive, but English requires it. |
| 1 | Defense verb | "was-making-defense" | "began to make his defense" | "began his defense" | Adopted LOGOS: "began to make his defense" -- the imperfect can indicate ingressive action; LOGOS and PNEUMA both capture this, but LOGOS is more complete. |
| 2 | "blessed" vs "fortunate" | "I-have-considered myself blessed" | "I consider myself blessed" | "I consider myself fortunate" | Adopted "blessed" (EMET/LOGOS). Greek makarios carries theological weight better rendered "blessed" than "fortunate." |
| 2 | Accusers identified | "by [the] Jews" | "by the Jews" | "by the Jewish leaders" | Adopted "by the Jews" (EMET/LOGOS). PNEUMA's "Jewish leaders" is interpretive; the Greek reads simply Ioudaioi. |
| 2 | Sentence structure | Verse 2 ends with "today" then verse 3 begins | Verses 2-3 combined into one flowing sentence | Verses 2-3 as separate sentences | Adopted LOGOS structure: verses 2-3 flow as one sentence, reflecting the Greek periodic sentence. |
| 3 | "knower" vs "expert" vs "well acquainted" | "a-knower of all the customs and also questions" | "an expert in all the customs and controversies" | "well acquainted with all the customs and controversies" | Adopted PNEUMA's "well acquainted" for gnōstēn but LOGOS's "controversies" for zētēmatōn. The composite reads naturally. |
| 3 | "I beg" vs "I ask" | "I-beg you to-hear me patiently" | "I beg you to hear me patiently" | "I ask you to listen to me patiently" | Adopted "I beg" (EMET/LOGOS). Greek deomai is stronger than a simple request. |
| 4 | Phrasing of "manner of life" | "The manner-of-life of-me therefore from youth" | "My manner of life from my youth" | "The way I have lived from my youth" | Adopted LOGOS: "My manner of life from my youth" -- preserves the Greek noun bios while reading naturally. |
| 4 | "all the Jews know" | "all the Jews know" | "all the Jews know" | "is well known to all the Jews" | Adopted active voice (EMET/LOGOS). PNEUMA's passive restructuring departs from the Greek syntax. |
| 5 | "knowing me beforehand from the first" | "knowing-me-beforehand from [the] first, if they-would-be-willing to-testify" | "having known me from the first -- if they are willing to testify" | "They have known me for a long time and can testify, if they are willing" | Adopted LOGOS: "having known me from the first -- if they are willing to testify." Preserves the participial structure and conditional. |
| 5 | "sect" vs "party" | "the strictest sect" | "the strictest sect" | "the strictest party" | Adopted "sect" (EMET/LOGOS). Hairesis in Acts is conventionally rendered "sect." |
| 6 | Verb phrasing | "I-have-stood being-judged" | "I stand being judged" | "I stand on trial" | Adopted LOGOS: "I stand being judged" -- PNEUMA's "on trial" is idiomatic but loses the Greek participle. EMET's perfect tense is possible but present tense is more natural here. |
| 6 | "promise made by God to our fathers" vs "ancestors" | "to our fathers" | "to our fathers" | "to our ancestors" | Adopted "fathers" (EMET/LOGOS), which is the traditional rendering of pateras in this context. |
| 7 | "in earnestness" vs "earnestly" vs "straining toward" | "in-earnestness night and day serving hope to-attain" | "earnestly serving God night and day, hope to attain" | "straining toward as they worship God earnestly, day and night" | Adopted LOGOS: "earnestly serving God night and day, hope to attain." PNEUMA's "straining toward" is interpretive; LOGOS preserves the participial structure. |
| 7 | Address to king | "O-king" | "O king!" | "Your Majesty" | Adopted "O king" (EMET/LOGOS). Greek basileu is a vocative; "Your Majesty" is an English upgrading not in the text. |
| 8 | Rhetorical question form | "Why [is it] judged unbelievable among you if God raises [the] dead?" | "Why is it judged incredible among you if God raises the dead?" | "Why should any of you consider it unbelievable that God raises the dead?" | Adopted LOGOS: "Why is it judged incredible among you if God raises the dead?" Preserves the passive verb krinetai and the conditional ei. |
| 9 | "the Nazorean" vs "the Nazarene" vs "of Nazareth" | "Jesus the Nazorean" | "Jesus the Nazarene" | "Jesus of Nazareth" | Adopted EMET: "Jesus the Nazorean." Sinaiticus reads ΝΑΖΩΡΑΙΟΥ, which is specifically "Nazorean," not "Nazarene." This is the form consistently used in Acts. |
| 9 | "I thought to myself" vs "I myself thought" | "I therefore thought to-myself [that] against the name..." | "I myself indeed thought it necessary to do many things in opposition to..." | "I myself was once convinced that I should do everything possible to oppose..." | Adopted LOGOS: "I myself indeed thought it necessary to do many things in opposition to." Closest to the Greek structure. PNEUMA's "everything possible" expands beyond the text. |
| 10 | "saints" vs "God's people" | "many of-the saints" | "many of the saints" | "many of God's people" | Adopted "saints" (EMET/LOGOS). Greek hagioi is literally "holy ones / saints." |
| 10 | "I cast down a vote" vs "I cast my vote" | "I-cast-down a-vote [against them]" | "I cast my vote against them" | "I cast my vote against them" | Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA: "I cast my vote against them." English idiom; the "cast down" is overly literal for katēnegka. |
| 11 | Structure and intensity | "many-times punishing them, I-was-compelling [them] to-blaspheme, and exceedingly being-enraged" | "punishing them often, I was compelling them to blaspheme; and being exceedingly enraged" | "I had them punished again and again...trying to force them to blaspheme. So obsessed was I" | Adopted LOGOS structure. PNEUMA's "so obsessed" interprets perissōs emmainomenos too freely; EMET is overly hyphenated. |
| 11 | "cities outside" vs "foreign cities" | "the cities outside" | "foreign cities" | "foreign cities" | Adopted "foreign cities" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). Greek exō more naturally renders as "foreign" in this context. |
| 12 | Opening phrase | "In which-things [while] going to Damascus" | "While so engaged, as I was journeying to Damascus" | "It was on one such mission that I was traveling to Damascus" | Adopted LOGOS: "While so engaged, as I was journeying to Damascus." PNEUMA's "one such mission" adds narrative framing not in the Greek. |
| 13 | Light description | "from-heaven above the brightness of-the sun, having-shone-around me a-light" | "a light from heaven, surpassing the brightness of the sun, shining around me" | "a light from heaven -- brighter than the sun -- blazing around me" | Adopted LOGOS: "a light from heaven, surpassing the brightness of the sun, shining around me." Best balance of accuracy and readability. PNEUMA's "blazing" intensifies beyond the Greek. |
| 13 | Companions | "the-ones with me traveling-together" | "those journeying with me" | "my companions on the road" | Adopted LOGOS: "those journeying with me." Preserves the participial structure. |
| 14 | "all of us having fallen down" | "all-of-us having-fallen-down to the ground" | "when we had all fallen to the ground" | "We all fell to the ground" | Adopted LOGOS: "And when we had all fallen to the ground." Temporal clause is clearer in English. |
| 14 | Goads proverb | "hard for-you to-kick against goads" | "hard for you to kick against the goads" | "You are only hurting yourself by fighting against the goad" | Adopted LOGOS: "It is hard for you to kick against the goads." PNEUMA's expansion ("you are only hurting yourself") adds interpretive material. LOGOS preserves the proverbial form. |
| 16 | "rise up and stand" vs "get up" | "rise-up and stand upon your feet" | "rise and stand upon your feet" | "get up. Stand on your feet" | Adopted LOGOS: "rise and stand upon your feet." "Get up" is too colloquial for this theophanic context. |
| 16 | Purpose clause | "for unto this I-appeared to-you, to-appoint you [as] a-servant and a-witness" | "for I have appeared to you for this purpose, to appoint you as a servant and a witness" | "I have appeared to you to appoint you as my servant and witness" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "my" (not in Greek). EMET's structure is awkward in English. |
| 16 | "things in which you saw me / I shall appear" | "of-the-things [in] which you-saw me and of-the-things [in] which I-shall-appear to-you" | "of the things in which you have seen me and of the things in which I will appear to you" | "of what you have already seen and of what I will yet reveal to you" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "reveal" interprets ophthēsomai ("I will appear") too freely. |
| 17 | "delivering" vs "rescuing" | "delivering you from the people and from the nations" | "delivering you from the people and from the Gentiles" | "I will rescue you from your own people and from the Gentiles" | Adopted LOGOS: "delivering you from the people and from the Gentiles." The participle exairoumenos is better as "delivering." PNEUMA's "your own people" adds a possessive. "Gentiles" preferred over "nations" for ethnōn in this missionary context. |
| 18 | "authority" vs "dominion" | "the authority of Satan" | "the authority of Satan" | "the dominion of Satan" | Adopted "authority" (EMET/LOGOS). Greek exousia is most precisely "authority." PNEUMA's "dominion" shifts the semantic range. |
| 18 | "a lot" vs "an inheritance" vs "a place" | "a-lot among the-ones having-been-sanctified" | "an inheritance among those who have been sanctified" | "a place among those who are made holy" | Adopted LOGOS: "an inheritance among those who have been sanctified." Greek klēron means "lot/inheritance"; "inheritance" is clearer. PNEUMA's "place" is too vague. |
| 19 | "From which thing" vs "Therefore" vs "So then" | "From-which-thing, O-king Agrippa" | "Therefore, King Agrippa" | "So then, King Agrippa" | Adopted LOGOS: "Therefore, King Agrippa." Hothen is a causal connector best rendered "therefore." |
| 20 | Repentance phrasing | "I-was-declaring to-repent and to-turn to God, doing works worthy of-repentance" | "that they should repent and turn to God, doing works worthy of repentance" | "that they should turn from their ways to God and prove their change of heart by how they live" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA paraphrases "works worthy of repentance" too freely as "prove their change of heart by how they live." |
| 21 | "on account of these things" vs "this is the reason" | "On-account-of these-things [the] Jews, having-seized me in the temple, were-attempting to-kill [me with their own]-hands" | "On account of these things the Jews, having seized me in the temple, were attempting to kill me" | "This is the reason the Jews seized me in the temple and tried to kill me" | Adopted LOGOS. EMET adds "with their own hands" (diacheirizomai does imply manual violence but the expansion is not needed). PNEUMA loses the participial structure. |
| 22 | "help from God" phrasing | "Having-obtained therefore help from God, until this day I-have-stood witnessing" | "Having therefore obtained the help that is from God, I stand to this day, testifying" | "But God has been my help to this very day, and so I stand here testifying" | Adopted LOGOS with minor adjustment: "Having therefore obtained help from God, I stand to this day, testifying." PNEUMA restructures the sentence. |
| 22 | "small and great" vs "powerful and humble" | "both to-small and to-great" | "both to small and great" | "both the powerful and the humble" | Adopted LOGOS: "both to small and great." PNEUMA reverses the Greek order and interprets rather than translates. |
| 22 | "nothing outside" vs "nothing beyond" | "saying nothing outside of-the-things" | "saying nothing beyond what" | "I say nothing beyond what" | Adopted LOGOS: "saying nothing beyond what." Natural English equivalent of ektos. |
| 23 | Christ's suffering | "whether the Christ [was] subject-to-suffering" | "that the Christ must suffer" | "that the Messiah would suffer" | Adopted LOGOS: "that the Christ must suffer." Greek pathētos means "subject to suffering" but the hoti clause requires a finite rendering. "Christ" preferred over "Messiah" as Christos is used as a title in Acts. |
| 23 | Resurrection phrasing | "first out-of [the] resurrection of [the] dead he-is-about to-proclaim light" | "being the first of the resurrection from the dead, he would proclaim light" | "he would be the first to rise from the dead, and that he would proclaim light" | Adopted LOGOS with adjustment: "being the first to rise from the dead, he would proclaim light." PNEUMA separates into two clauses; EMET is overly literal. |
| 23 | "people and nations" vs "people and Gentiles" | "both to-the people and to-the nations" | "both to the people and to the Gentiles" | "both to our people and to the Gentiles" | Adopted LOGOS: "both to the people and to the Gentiles." PNEUMA adds "our" not in the text. |
| 24 | Festus's speech verb | "Festus with-a-loud voice says" | "Festus said with a loud voice" | "Festus shouted" | Adopted LOGOS: "Festus said with a loud voice." The Greek phēsin is present tense (historic present); PNEUMA's "shouted" loses the speech verb. |
| 24 | "mad" vs "out of your mind" | "You-are-mad, Paul!" | "You are mad, Paul!" | "You are out of your mind, Paul!" | Adopted "mad" (EMET/LOGOS). Greek mainē is directly "you are mad." |
| 24 | "the many writings" vs "great learning" | "The many writings are-turning you to madness" | "Your great learning is driving you to madness" | "Your great learning is driving you insane" | Adopted LOGOS: "Your great learning is driving you to madness." Greek ta polla grammata can mean "many letters/writings" or "much learning"; "great learning" is the conventional English rendering. "Madness" preserves mania. |
| 25 | "I am not mad" vs "I am not insane" | "I-am not mad, most-excellent Festus, but words of-truth and of-soundness-of-mind I-am-uttering" | "I am not mad, most excellent Festus, but I am speaking words of truth and soundness of mind" | "I am not insane, most excellent Festus. What I am saying is the sober truth" | Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "sober truth" collapses "truth and soundness of mind" (two distinct concepts: alētheia and sōphrosynē). |
| 26 | Phrasing of Paul's confidence | "to-have-escaped-notice [of] him any of these-things I-am not persuaded" | "I am persuaded that none of these things escapes his notice" | "I am convinced that none of this has escaped his notice" | Adopted LOGOS: "I am persuaded that none of these things escapes his notice." PNEUMA's "convinced" is acceptable but "persuaded" (peithō) is more precise. |
| 26 | "not done in a corner" | "this is not having-been-done in a-corner" | "this has not been done in a corner" | "it was not done in some hidden corner" | Adopted LOGOS: "this has not been done in a corner." PNEUMA adds "hidden" and "some," which are interpretive. |
| 28 | Agrippa's reply | "In a-little [time] you-are-persuading me to-make [me] a-Christian" | "In a short time you are persuading me to become a Christian!" | "In so short a time, are you trying to make me a Christian?" | Adopted LOGOS: "In a short time you are persuading me to become a Christian!" PNEUMA restructures as a question; the Greek can be read either way, but the declarative/ironic reading is more consistent with the Sinaiticus text. |
| 29 | Paul's reply structure | "I-would-pray to God, both in a-little and in a-great [time], not only you but also all the-ones hearing me today to-become such-as also I am, except [for] these bonds" | "I would pray to God, whether in a short time or in a long time, that not only you but also all who hear me today might become such as I am -- except for these chains" | "Whether it takes a short time or a long time, I pray to God that not only you but everyone listening today would become what I am -- except for these chains" | Adopted LOGOS. Best renders the optative mood (euchaimen) and the rhetorical echo of en oligō / en megalō. PNEUMA front-loads the temporal clause, losing the prayer's priority. |
| 29 | "bonds" vs "chains" | "these bonds" | "these chains" | "these chains" | Adopted "chains" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). Greek desmōn can be either; "chains" is more vivid and conventional in English. |
| 31 | "nothing worthy of death or bonds" | "Nothing worthy of-death or of-bonds is this man doing" | "This man is doing nothing worthy of death or of chains" | "This man has done nothing deserving death or imprisonment" | Adopted LOGOS: "This man is doing nothing worthy of death or of chains." PNEUMA's "imprisonment" interprets desmōn; EMET's word order is awkward. |
| 32 | "released" vs "set free" | "was-able to-have-been-released" | "could have been released" | "could have been set free" | Adopted LOGOS: "could have been released." Apolelysthai is precisely "released." PNEUMA's "set free" is slightly more interpretive. |

---

-- SOPHIA
